blocks:
Ayahs

وَٱلذَّٰرِيَـٰتِ ذَرْوًا (١)
By the scattering [winds] that scatter [the clouds]; (1)
فَٱلْحَـٰمِلَـٰتِ وِقْرًا (٢)
by the [rain] bearing [clouds] laden [with water]; (2)
فَٱلْجَـٰرِيَـٰتِ يُسْرًا (٣)
by [the ships] which move gently [on the sea]; (3)
فَٱلْمُقَسِّمَـٰتِ أَمْرًا (٤)
by [the angels] who dispense [livelihood] by [His] command: (4)
إِنَّمَا تُوعَدُونَ لَصَادِقٌ (٥)
indeed what you are promised is true, (5)
وَإِنَّ ٱلدِّينَ لَوَٰقِعٌ (٦)
and indeed the retribution will surely come to pass! (6)
وَٱلسَّمَآءِ ذَاتِ ٱلْحُبُكِ (٧)
By the sky full of adornment [with stars], (7)
إِنَّكُمْ لَفِى قَوْلٍ مُّخْتَلِفٍ (٨)
indeed you are of different opinions! (8)
يُؤْفَكُ عَنْهُ مَنْ أُفِكَ (٩)
He who has been turned away [from the truth] is turned away from it. (9)
قُتِلَ ٱلْخَرَّٰصُونَ (١٠)
Perish the liars, (10)
ٱلَّذِينَ هُمْ فِى غَمْرَةٍ سَاهُونَ (١١)
who are heedless in a stupor! (11)
يَسْـَٔلُونَ أَيَّانَ يَوْمُ ٱلدِّينِ (١٢)
They ask, ‘When will be the Day of Retribution?’ (12)
يَوْمَ هُمْ عَلَى ٱلنَّارِ يُفْتَنُونَ (١٣)
It is the day when they will be tormented in the Fire, (13)
ذُوقُوا۟ فِتْنَتَكُمْ هَـٰذَا ٱلَّذِى كُنتُم بِهِۦ تَسْتَعْجِلُونَ (١٤)
[and will be told]: ‘Taste your torment. This is what you used to hasten.’ (14)
إِنَّ ٱلْمُتَّقِينَ فِى جَنَّـٰتٍ وَعُيُونٍ (١٥)
Indeed the Godwary will be amid gardens and springs, (15)
ءَاخِذِينَ مَآ ءَاتَىٰهُمْ رَبُّهُمْ ۚ إِنَّهُمْ كَانُوا۟ قَبْلَ ذَٰلِكَ مُحْسِنِينَ (١٦)
receiving what their Lord has given them, for they had been virtuous aforetime. (16)
كَانُوا۟ قَلِيلًا مِّنَ ٱلَّيْلِ مَا يَهْجَعُونَ (١٧)
They used to sleep a little during the night, (17)
وَبِٱلْأَسْحَارِ هُمْ يَسْتَغْفِرُونَ (١٨)
and at dawns they would plead for forgiveness, (18)
وَفِىٓ أَمْوَٰلِهِمْ حَقٌّ لِّلسَّآئِلِ وَٱلْمَحْرُومِ (١٩)
and there was a share in their wealth for the beggar and the deprived. (19)

(Commentary)

The prophetic mission (الدعوة النبوية) used to call the pagans to the monotheism of Lordship (توحيد الربوبية) — the belief that Allah alone is the Sustainer and Governor of all affairs — and to the fact that Allah the Exalted is their Lord and the Lord of all things. This call was conducted through the means of warning (الإنذار) and glad tidings (التبشير), specifically emphasizing warning.

The warning was of Allah’s punishment in this world for the deniers—the punishment of extirpation (عذاب الاستئصال)—and in the Hereafter with eternal punishment on the Day of Resurrection (يوم القيامة). This latter point is the primary pillar for the success of the mission; for if it were not for the reckoning (الحساب) and the recompense (الجزاء) on the Day of Resurrection, faith in Divine Oneness (الوحدانية) and Prophethood (النبوة) would be futile (لغو) and without effect.

By taking gods besides Allah, the glorified, the polytheists intensified their denial of the foundations of monotheism (التوحيد), prophethood, and the return (المعاد) — the restoration of life after death for judgment. They were obstinate in denying the Return, persistent in its negation, and mocking it through every possible means, as they perceived that its invalidation would lead to the invalidation of the other two foundations.

This Surah mentions the Return and their denial of it; it begins with it and concludes upon it. However, it does not address it in terms of its own essence, as the discourse proceeds in other places of His speech, but rather in terms of it being the Day of Recompense (يوم الجزاء) and that Allah, who promised it to them, is their Lord; He is the one who promised it, and His promise is a truth (صدق) in which there is no doubt.

Therefore, when the discourse transitions to arguing for it, it employs the proofs of monotheism from the signs (آيات) of the earth, the heavens, and the souls (الأنفس), as well as the punishments Allah inflicted upon past nations following their call to monotheism and their denial of His messengers. This is solely to establish monotheism through them, thereby establishing the Day of Recompense which Allah promised—and Allah does not fail His promise—and which the prophetic mission announced. Thus, their denial of the recompense is refuted, a denial they used as a means to invalidate the religion of monotheism and the message of the Messenger, since faith in it would otherwise become futile and without effect, as previously indicated.

The Surah is Meccan, as evidenced by the context of its verses, and there is no disagreement on this. Among its most luminous verses is His saying, the Exalted: وَمَا خَلَقْتُ الْجِنَّ وَالْإِنْسَ إِلَّا لِيَعْبُدُونِ — "I did not create the jinn and the humans except that they may worship Me." (51:56).

The section of verses we have presented is the opening of the discourse, in which it is mentioned that the recompense they are promised is a truth, and that their denial and obstinacy regarding it is mere conjecture (تخرص). It then describes the Day of Recompense and the state of the God-conscious (المتقين) and the deniers therein.

His saying, the Exalted: وَالذَّارِياتِ ذَرْواً فَالْحامِلاتِ وِقْراً فَالْجارِياتِ يُسْراً فَالْمُقَسِّماتِ أَمْراً — "By the [winds] that scatter [the dust] far and wide, by the [clouds] that bear the burden [of rain], by the [ships] that sail with ease, by the [angels] who dispense [the provisions] by [His] command," (51:1-4). الذاريات (the scatterers) is the plural of al-dhāriyah, from the expression: "the wind scattered (ذرت) the dust" when it makes it fly. Al-wiqr (الوقر), with a kasrah on the first letter and sukūn on the second, refers to a heavy load carried on the back or in the womb.

In these verses, there is oath after oath, providing emphasis after emphasis for the subject of the oath, which is the recompense for deeds. His saying: وَالذَّارِياتِ ذَرْواً is an oath by the winds that stir up the dust. His saying: فَالْحامِلاتِ وِقْراً, with the particle fā’ (ف) denoting sequence and order, is conjoined to "the scatterers" and is an oath by the clouds carrying the heavy weight of water. His saying: فَالْجارِياتِ يُسْراً is conjoined to it and is an oath by the ships sailing in the seas with ease and facility.

His saying: فَالْمُقَسِّماتِ أَمْراً is conjoined to what preceded it and is an oath by the angels (الملائكة) who act by His command, distributing it according to their varying stations. For the command (الأمر) of the Possessor of the Throne regarding creation and governance is one; but when a group of angels carries it out according to their diverse tasks, the command branches out and is divided by their division. Then, if a group lower than the first group carries it, it is divided a second time by their division, and so on, until it reaches the angels directly responsible for particular cosmic events (الحوادث الكونية الجزئية), where it divides by their division and multiplies by their multiplication.

The four verses—as you see—point to the totality of governance (عامة التدبير), as they mention a model of how affairs are governed on land, which is "the scatterers far and wide"; a model of how affairs are governed at sea, which is "those that sail with ease"; and a model of how affairs are governed in the atmosphere, which is "those that bear the burden." All of this is completed by the angels, who are the intermediaries of governance (وسائط التدبير), and they are "those who dispense the command."

Thus, the verses carry the meaning of saying: I swear by all the causes (الأسباب) through which the matter of governance in the world is completed, that such-and-forth is true. It has been narrated through both Shi'a and Sunni chains from Ali, upon whom be the best of peace (عليه أفضل السلام), that he interpreted these four verses as mentioned above.

It is reported from Fakhr al-Razi in al-Tafsir al-Kabir that it is more likely that all four verses refer to the winds, for they scatter the dust, carry the heavy clouds, move through the air with ease, and distribute the clouds over the regions of the earth.

The truth is that what he considered likely is far-fetched, and the meaning previously mentioned is more eloquent than what he stated.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّما تُوعَدُونَ لَصادِقٌ وَإِنَّ الدِّينَ لَواقِعٌ — "Indeed what you are promised is true, and indeed the Retribution will surely take place." (51:5-6). The word mā (ما) is a relative pronoun, and the pronoun referring back to it is omitted, meaning "that which you are promised." Or it is an infinitive particle (مصدرية), and تُوعَدُونَ (you are promised) is from "the promise" (الوعد), as supported by His saying: وَإِنَّ الدِّينَ لَواقِعٌ, which encompasses absolute recompense. It is also said to be from "the threat" (الإيعاد), as supported by His saying: فَذَكِّرْ بِالْقُرْآنِ مَنْ يَخافُ وَعِيدِ — "So admonish by the Quran him who fears My threat." (50:45).

Describing the promise as "truthful" (صادق) is a metaphorical attribution (مجاز في النسبة), as in His saying: فِي عِيشَةٍ راضِيَةٍ — "in a pleasant life" (69:21), or "truthful" means "possessing truth," as has been said regarding the same verse. الدين (the Religion) here means the recompense (الجزاء).

In any case, His saying: إِنَّما تُوعَدُونَ لَصادِقٌ is the response to the oath (جواب القسم), and His saying: وَإِنَّ الدِّينَ لَواقِعٌ is conjoined to it, serving as an explanation. The meaning is: I swear by such-and-forth that what you are promised—which is what the Quran or the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) promises them by what was revealed to him—regarding the Day of Resurrection and that Allah will recompense them therein for their deeds, if good then good, and if evil then evil, is indeed true, and the recompense will surely occur.

His saying, the Exalted: وَالسَّماءِ ذاتِ الْحُبُكِ — "By the sky with its [starry] tracks," (51:7). الحبك (the tracks) means beauty and adornment, and it also means a well-proportioned creation. It can also be the plural of habīkah or hibāk, meaning a path (الطريقة), like the paths that appear on water when it ripples and breaks from the wind passing over it.

According to the first meaning, the sense is: I swear by the heaven possessing beauty and adornment, similar to His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّا زَيَّنَّا السَّماءَ الدُّنْيا بِزِينَةٍ الْكَواكِبِ — "Indeed We have adorned the lowest heaven with the adornment of the stars," (37:6). According to the second: I swear by the heaven possessing a well-proportioned creation, similar to His saying: وَالسَّماءَ بَنَيْناها بِأَيْدٍ — "We have built the sky with might," (51:47). According to the third: I swear by the heaven possessing paths, similar to His saying: وَلَقَدْ خَلَقْنا فَوْقَكُمْ سَبْعَ طَرائِقَ — "Certainly We have created above you seven paths," (23:17).

Perhaps the third meaning is the most apparent due to its suitability to the response of the oath, which is the disagreement of the people and the scattering of their paths, just as the previous oaths: "By the scatterers far and wide," etc., shared the meaning of flowing and traveling, suitable to their response: "Indeed what you are promised," etc., which contains the meaning of returning to Allah and traveling toward Him.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّكُمْ لَفِي قَوْلٍ مُخْتَلِفٍ يُؤْفَكُ عَنْهُ مَنْ أُفِكَ — "you are surely in a discordant view. He who is perverted is perverted from it." (51:8-9). Discordant view (القول المختلف) is that which is contradictory and refutes itself. Since the discourse is about establishing the truthfulness of the Quran, the mission, or the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) regarding what he promised them of the Resurrection and Recompense, the "discordant view"—most likely—refers to their differing statements regarding the Quran for the purpose of denying what it establishes. Sometimes they say: it is sorcery and the one who brought it is a sorcerer; sometimes they say: it is a trance and the one who brought it is possessed; sometimes they say: it is the suggestion of devils and the one who brought it is a soothsayer; sometimes they say: it is poetry and the one who brought it is a poet; sometimes that it is a fabrication; sometimes they say: only a human teaches him; and sometimes they say: fables of the ancients which he has written down.

His saying: يُؤْفَكُ عَنْهُ مَنْ أُفِكَ. Al-ifk (الإفك) means turning away (الصرف). The pronoun in "from it" (عَنْهُ) refers to the Book in terms of its inclusion of the promise of Resurrection and Recompense. The meaning is: he who is turned away is turned away from the Quran. It is also said that the pronoun refers to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), meaning: he who is turned away is turned away from believing in him. You have seen that the previous meaning is more in accord with the context, even if the ultimate result of both meanings is the same.

It is narrated from some that the pronoun in "from it" refers to "what you are promised" or to "the Religion." Allah first swore by the scatterers and others that the Resurrection and Recompense are true, then He swore by the heaven that they are in a discordant view regarding its occurrence—some being doubters and others deniers. Then He said: he who is perverted is perverted from acknowledging the matter of Resurrection and Recompense. This view is close to the previous one.

From others: the pronoun refers to "discordant view," and the particle "from" (عن) denotes causality, as in His saying, the Exalted: وَما نَحْنُ بِتارِكِي آلِهَتِنا عَنْ قَوْلِكَ — "we are not going to abandon our gods for your saying," (11:53). Thus, the sentence would be an attribute of "view," and the meaning: you are in a discordant view because of which he who is perverted is perverted. This is a fine view.

It is also said: the pronoun in "you" (إِنَّكُمْ) refers to both the Muslim and the disbeliever, so the discordant view would mean the view of the Muslims regarding the occurrence of the Resurrection and Recompense, and the view of the disbelievers regarding its non-occurrence. Perhaps the context does not suit this. Other weak views have been mentioned which are of no use to address.

His saying, the Exalted: قُتِلَ الْخَرَّاصُونَ الَّذِينَ هُمْ فِي غَمْرَةٍ ساهُونَ يَسْئَلُونَ أَيَّانَ يَوْمُ الدِّينِ — "Perish the liars! —those who are in a stupor of heedlessness. They ask, ‘When is the Day of Retribution?’" (51:10-12). The root of al-kharṣ (الخرص) is speaking by conjecture and guesswork without knowledge. Because speaking without knowledge carries the risk of lying, the liar is called a kharrāṣ (conjecturer). It is most likely that the "conjecturers" in the verse refers to those who speak without knowledge or proof, those who plunge into the matter of Resurrection and Recompense, denying it without knowledge.

In His saying: قُتِلَ الْخَرَّاصُونَ, there is an invocation against them for death, which is a metaphor for a type of expulsion and deprivation from success; this is what the interpretation of those who explained it as "cursing" (اللعن) boils down to.

His saying: الَّذِينَ هُمْ فِي غَمْرَةٍ ساهُونَ. Al-ghamrah (الغمرة)—as mentioned by al-Raghib—is the mass of water that covers its bed, and it is used as a metaphor for the ignorance that overwhelms its possessor. Al-sahw (السهو) is said to mean absolute heedlessness (الغفلة).

The meaning of the verse as it describes the conjecturers is: those who are in an ignorance that has surrounded them, heedless of the reality of what they have been informed of.

His saying: يَسْئَلُونَ أَيَّانَ يَوْمُ الدِّينِ. The plural pronoun refers to the conjecturers; it is a statement they made by way of seeking haste in mockery, like their saying: مَتى هذَا الْوَعْدُ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ صادِقِينَ — "When will this promise be fulfilled, if you are truthful?" (36:48).

The question using ayyāna (أَيَّانَ)—which is designated for asking about the time of its object—regarding the Day of Recompense, which is clearly a time itself, is only by way of considering that the Day of Recompense, being promised, is treated like temporal events. Thus, it is asked about as temporal events are asked about with ayyāna and matā (when), just as it is said: "When is the day of the festival?" because it is an event treated as a temporal thing. So it has been said.

It is possible that it is from the expansion (التوسع) of the meaning of adverbiality (الظرفية), by considering the specific attributes of the adverb as an adverb itself by way of expansion. Thus, the question about the time of a time is a question of whether it is after which time or before which time? As it is said: "When is the day of the festival?" and the answer is that it is after ten days, for example, or before such-and-forth day. This is an expansion common in custom, not exclusive to the speech of the Arabs, and there is much of it in the Quran.

His saying, the Exalted: يَوْمَ هُمْ عَلَى النَّارِ يُفْتَنُونَ — "The day when they are tormented in the Fire." (51:13). The plural pronoun refers to the conjecturers. Al-fatn (الفتن) originally means putting gold into the fire to manifest its quality, then it was used for absolute burning and punishment. The adverbial phrase is related to an omitted verb or is a subject (مبتدأ). The verse is an answer to their question, in which it diverts from explaining the specific time of the Day of Recompense to explaining its description and pointing to their state therein, because its time is from the Unseen (الغيب) which none knows but Allah. He said: لا يُجَلِّيها لِوَقْتِها إِلَّا هُوَ — "None shall manifest it at its time but He." (7:187).

The estimation and meaning of the verse is: the Day of Recompense occurs, or it is happening, on the day when they—the conjecturers—are punished or burned in the Fire.

His saying, the Exalted: ذُوقُوا فِتْنَتَكُمْ هذَا الَّذِي كُنْتُمْ بِهِ تَسْتَعْجِلُونَ — "‘Taste your punishment! This is what you used to seek to hasten.’" (51:14). This is a narration of a speech from Him, the Exalted, or from the angels by His command to the conjecturers while they are being tormented in the Fire on that day.

The meaning is: it is said to them, "Taste the punishment that is specific to you. This punishment is what you used to seek to hasten when you said in haste and mockery: 'When is the Day of Recompense?'"

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ الْمُتَّقِينَ فِي جَنَّاتٍ وَعُيُونٍ — "Indeed the God-conscious will be amid gardens and springs," (51:15). This is an explanation of the state of the God-conscious (المتقين) — those who are wary of Allah's boundaries and protect themselves from His punishment — on the Day of Recompense, after describing the state of those conjecturers.

The indefiniteness (تنكير) of "gardens" and "springs" is to point to the greatness of their status, as if they are such that describers cannot describe them. The springs have been conjoined to the gardens in their adverbiality by way of expansion.

His saying, the Exalted: آخِذِينَ ما آتاهُمْ رَبُّهُمْ إِنَّهُمْ كانُوا قَبْلَ ذلِكَ مُحْسِنِينَ — "receiving what their Lord has given them. Indeed they were virtuous before that." (51:16). That is, accepting what their Lord, who is Compassionate toward them, has given them, being pleased with Him and with what He has given, as suggested by the specific expression of "receiving" (الأخذ) and "giving" (الإيتاء) and the attribution of giving to their Lord.

His saying: إِنَّهُمْ كانُوا قَبْلَ ذلِكَ مُحْسِنِينَ is a justification for what preceded it; i.e., their state is such because they were before that—meaning in the world—possessors of excellence (إحسان) in their deeds, i.e., possessors of righteous deeds (أعمال حسنة).

His saying, the Exalted: كانُوا قَلِيلاً مِنَ اللَّيْلِ ما يَهْجَعُونَ — "They used to sleep but little of the night," (51:17). These verses are an explanation of their excellence. Al-hajū‘ (الهجوع) is sleeping at night, and it is said: a short sleep.

It is possible that mā (ما) is extra (زائدة) and يَهْجَعُونَ is the predicate of kānū, and قَلِيلاً is an adverb related to it, meaning "in a little time," or an attribute of an omitted absolute object, meaning "a little sleeping," and مِنَ اللَّيْلِ is related to "little." The meaning would be: they used to sleep for a little time of the night, or they used to sleep the night a little sleep.

It is also possible that it is a relative pronoun (موصولة) with the returning pronoun omitted, and قَلِيلاً is the predicate of kānū and the relative clause is its subject. The meaning: little was the part of the night in which they slept.

It is also possible that it is an infinitive particle (مصدرية), and the infinitive formed from it and its object is the subject of His saying قَلِيلاً, which is the predicate of كانُوا.

In any case, the "little of the night" is either taken in comparison to the total time of each night, implying that they sleep a little time of every night and pray most of it; or it is taken in comparison to the total number of nights, implying that they sleep in a few of the nights and stand for prayer in most of them; i.e., they do not miss the night prayer except in a few nights.

His saying, the Exalted: وَبِالْأَسْحارِ هُمْ يَسْتَغْفِرُونَ — "and at dawns they would plead for forgiveness." (51:18). That is, they ask Allah for forgiveness for their sins. It is said: the meaning of seeking forgiveness (الاستغفار) is prayer (الصلاة), but it is as you see.

His saying, the Exalted: وَفِي أَمْوالِهِمْ حَقٌّ لِلسَّائِلِ وَالْمَحْرُومِ — "And in their wealth there was a share for the beggar and the deprived." (51:19). The two previous verses explain the specific nature of their conduct in relation to Allah, the Glorified, which is standing at night and seeking forgiveness at dawn. This verse explains the specific nature of their conduct in relation to people, which is giving to the beggar and the deprived.

Specifying the right of the beggar and the deprived as being "in their wealth"—even though if it were established, it would be established in all wealth—is proof that the meaning is that they see, through the purity of their primordial nature (الفطرة), that there is a right for these two in their wealth, so they act accordingly to spread mercy and prefer excellence.

The beggar (السائل) is the one who asks for a gift by manifesting need, and the deprived (المحروم) is the one who has been denied provision, whose effort in seeking it did not succeed, yet he does not ask out of self-respect (تعفف).

(Narrative Discussion)

In Tafsir al-Qummi: My father narrated to me from Ibn Abi ‘Umayr from Jamil from Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him) regarding the saying of the Exalted: وَالذَّارِياتِ ذَرْواً. He said: "Ibn al-Kawwa’ asked the Commander of the Faithful (peace be upon him) about وَالذَّارِياتِ ذَرْواً, and he said: 'The wind.' About فَالْحامِلاتِ وِقْراً, he said: 'They are the clouds.' About فَالْجارِياتِ يُسْراً, he said: 'They are the ships.' And about فَالْمُقَسِّماتِ أَمْراً, he said: 'The angels.'"

I say: The hadith is also narrated through Sunni chains as in Ruh al-Ma'ani.

In al-Durr al-Manthur: ‘Abd al-Razzaq, al-Faryabi, Sa'id ibn Mansur, al-Harith ibn Abi Usamah, Ibn Jarir, Ibn al-Mundhir, Ibn Abi Hatim, Ibn al-Anbari in al-Masahif, and al-Hakim (who authenticated it), and al-Bayhaqi in Shu'ab al-Iman narrated through various chains from Ali ibn Abi Talib regarding His saying: وَالذَّارِياتِ ذَرْواً, he said: "The winds." فَالْحامِلاتِ وِقْراً, he said: "The clouds." فَالْجارِياتِ يُسْراً, he said: "The ships." فَالْمُقَسِّماتِ أَمْراً, he said: "The angels."

In al-Majma': Abu Ja'far and Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon them) said: "It is not permissible for anyone to swear except by Allah the Exalted, but Allah swears by whatever He wills of His creation."

In al-Durr al-Manthur: Ibn Mani' narrated from Ali ibn Abi Talib that he was asked about His saying: وَالسَّماءِ ذاتِ الْحُبُكِ, and he said: "Possessing a beautiful creation."

I say: The like of it is narrated in al-Majma', and its wording is: "It is said: possessing beauty and adornment: from Ali (peace be upon him)." And in Jawami' al-Jami', its wording is: "And from Ali (peace be upon him): its beauty and its adornment."

In some reports: regarding His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّكُمْ لَفِي قَوْلٍ مُخْتَلِفٍ يُؤْفَكُ عَنْهُ مَنْ أُفِكَ, it is applied to the Wilayah (الولاية) — the divinely appointed authority and guardianship.

In al-Majma', regarding His saying, the Exalted: كانُوا قَلِيلاً مِنَ اللَّيْلِ ما يَهْجَعُونَ, it is said its meaning is: "Rarely did a night pass them by except that they prayed in it." This is what is narrated from Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him).

In it, regarding His saying, the Exalted: وَبِالْأَسْحارِ هُمْ يَسْتَغْفِرُونَ, Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him) said: "They used to seek forgiveness from Allah in the Witr prayer seventy times at dawn."

In al-Durr al-Manthur: Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Anas, who said: The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: "The end of the night for the night prayer (التهجد) is more beloved to me than its beginning, because Allah says: وَبِالْأَسْحارِ هُمْ يَسْتَغْفِرُونَ."

In it: Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Ibn ‘Umar from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) regarding His saying: وَبِالْأَسْحارِ هُمْ يَسْتَغْفِرُونَ, he said: "They pray."

I say: Perhaps the interpretation of seeking forgiveness as prayer is because the Witr prayer includes it, just as prayer is intended by "Quran" in His saying: وَقُرْآنَ الْفَجْرِ إِنَّ قُرْآنَ الْفَجْرِ كانَ مَشْهُوداً — "and the recital of dawn. Indeed the recital of dawn is witnessed." (17:78).

In Tafsir al-Qummi, regarding His saying, the Exalted: وَفِي أَمْوالِهِمْ حَقٌّ لِلسَّائِلِ وَالْمَحْرُومِ, he said: "The beggar (السائل) is the one who asks, and the deprived (المحروم) is the one who has been prevented from [the fruits of] his labor."

In al-Tahdhib, with its chain to Safwan al-Jammal from Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him) regarding the verse, he said: "The deprived is the muḥāraf—the one who has been deprived of the labor of his hand in buying and selling."

He said: In another narration from Abu Ja'far and Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon them), they said: "The deprived is the man who has no defect in his intellect, yet provision is not expanded for him, and he is a muḥāraf."

Ayahs

وَفِى ٱلْأَرْضِ ءَايَـٰتٌ لِّلْمُوقِنِينَ (٢٠)
In the earth are signs for those who have conviction, (20)
وَفِىٓ أَنفُسِكُمْ ۚ أَفَلَا تُبْصِرُونَ (٢١)
and in your souls [as well]. Will you not then perceive? (21)
وَفِى ٱلسَّمَآءِ رِزْقُكُمْ وَمَا تُوعَدُونَ (٢٢)
And in the sky is your provision and what you are promised. (22)
فَوَرَبِّ ٱلسَّمَآءِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ إِنَّهُۥ لَحَقٌّ مِّثْلَ مَآ أَنَّكُمْ تَنطِقُونَ (٢٣)
By the Lord of the sky and the earth, it is indeed the truth, just as [it is a fact that] you speak. (23)
هَلْ أَتَىٰكَ حَدِيثُ ضَيْفِ إِبْرَٰهِيمَ ٱلْمُكْرَمِينَ (٢٤)
Did you receive the story of Abraham’s honoured guests? (24)
إِذْ دَخَلُوا۟ عَلَيْهِ فَقَالُوا۟ سَلَـٰمًا ۖ قَالَ سَلَـٰمٌ قَوْمٌ مُّنكَرُونَ (٢٥)
When they entered into his presence, they said, ‘Peace!’ ‘Peace!’ He answered, ‘[You are] an unfamiliar folk.’ (25)
فَرَاغَ إِلَىٰٓ أَهْلِهِۦ فَجَآءَ بِعِجْلٍ سَمِينٍ (٢٦)
Then he retired to his family and brought a fat [roasted] calf, (26)
فَقَرَّبَهُۥٓ إِلَيْهِمْ قَالَ أَلَا تَأْكُلُونَ (٢٧)
and put it near them. He said, ‘Will you not eat?’ (27)
فَأَوْجَسَ مِنْهُمْ خِيفَةً ۖ قَالُوا۟ لَا تَخَفْ ۖ وَبَشَّرُوهُ بِغُلَـٰمٍ عَلِيمٍ (٢٨)
Then he felt a fear of them. They said, ‘Do not be afraid!’ and they gave him the good news of a wise son. (28)
فَأَقْبَلَتِ ٱمْرَأَتُهُۥ فِى صَرَّةٍ فَصَكَّتْ وَجْهَهَا وَقَالَتْ عَجُوزٌ عَقِيمٌ (٢٩)
Then his wife came forward crying [with joy]. She beat her face, and said, ‘A barren old woman!’ (29)
قَالُوا۟ كَذَٰلِكِ قَالَ رَبُّكِ ۖ إِنَّهُۥ هُوَ ٱلْحَكِيمُ ٱلْعَلِيمُ (٣٠)
They said, ‘So has your Lord said. Indeed He is the All-wise, the All-knowing.’ (30)
۞ قَالَ فَمَا خَطْبُكُمْ أَيُّهَا ٱلْمُرْسَلُونَ (٣١)
He said, ‘O messengers, what is now your errand?’ (31)
قَالُوٓا۟ إِنَّآ أُرْسِلْنَآ إِلَىٰ قَوْمٍ مُّجْرِمِينَ (٣٢)
They said, ‘We have been sent toward a guilty people, (32)
لِنُرْسِلَ عَلَيْهِمْ حِجَارَةً مِّن طِينٍ (٣٣)
that We may rain upon them stones of clay, (33)
مُّسَوَّمَةً عِندَ رَبِّكَ لِلْمُسْرِفِينَ (٣٤)
sent for the transgressors from your Lord. (34)
فَأَخْرَجْنَا مَن كَانَ فِيهَا مِنَ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ (٣٥)
So We picked out those who were in it of the faithful, (35)
فَمَا وَجَدْنَا فِيهَا غَيْرَ بَيْتٍ مِّنَ ٱلْمُسْلِمِينَ (٣٦)
but We did not find there other than one house of Muslims, (36)
وَتَرَكْنَا فِيهَآ ءَايَةً لِّلَّذِينَ يَخَافُونَ ٱلْعَذَابَ ٱلْأَلِيمَ (٣٧)
and We have left therein a sign for those who fear a painful punishment.’ (37)
وَفِى مُوسَىٰٓ إِذْ أَرْسَلْنَـٰهُ إِلَىٰ فِرْعَوْنَ بِسُلْطَـٰنٍ مُّبِينٍ (٣٨)
And in Moses [too there is a sign] when We sent him to Pharaoh with a manifest authority. (38)
فَتَوَلَّىٰ بِرُكْنِهِۦ وَقَالَ سَـٰحِرٌ أَوْ مَجْنُونٌ (٣٩)
But he turned away assured of his might, and said, ‘A magician or a crazy man!’ (39)
فَأَخَذْنَـٰهُ وَجُنُودَهُۥ فَنَبَذْنَـٰهُمْ فِى ٱلْيَمِّ وَهُوَ مُلِيمٌ (٤٠)
So We seized him and his hosts, and cast them into the sea, while he was blameworthy. (40)
وَفِى عَادٍ إِذْ أَرْسَلْنَا عَلَيْهِمُ ٱلرِّيحَ ٱلْعَقِيمَ (٤١)
And in ‘Ad when We unleashed upon them a barren wind. (41)
مَا تَذَرُ مِن شَىْءٍ أَتَتْ عَلَيْهِ إِلَّا جَعَلَتْهُ كَٱلرَّمِيمِ (٤٢)
It left nothing that it came upon without making it like decayed bones. (42)
وَفِى ثَمُودَ إِذْ قِيلَ لَهُمْ تَمَتَّعُوا۟ حَتَّىٰ حِينٍ (٤٣)
And in Thamud, when they were told, ‘Enjoy for a while.’ (43)
فَعَتَوْا۟ عَنْ أَمْرِ رَبِّهِمْ فَأَخَذَتْهُمُ ٱلصَّـٰعِقَةُ وَهُمْ يَنظُرُونَ (٤٤)
Then they defied the command of their Lord; so the thunderbolt seized them as they looked on. (44)
فَمَا ٱسْتَطَـٰعُوا۟ مِن قِيَامٍ وَمَا كَانُوا۟ مُنتَصِرِينَ (٤٥)
So they were neither able to rise up, nor to come to one another’s aid. (45)
وَقَوْمَ نُوحٍ مِّن قَبْلُ ۖ إِنَّهُمْ كَانُوا۟ قَوْمًا فَـٰسِقِينَ (٤٦)
And the people of Noah aforetime. Indeed, they were a transgressing lot. (46)
وَٱلسَّمَآءَ بَنَيْنَـٰهَا بِأَيْي۟دٍ وَإِنَّا لَمُوسِعُونَ (٤٧)
We have built the heaven with might, and indeed it is We who are its expanders. (47)
وَٱلْأَرْضَ فَرَشْنَـٰهَا فَنِعْمَ ٱلْمَـٰهِدُونَ (٤٨)
And We have spread out the earth, so how excellent spreaders We have been! (48)
وَمِن كُلِّ شَىْءٍ خَلَقْنَا زَوْجَيْنِ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَذَكَّرُونَ (٤٩)
In all things We have created pairs so that you may take admonition. (49)
فَفِرُّوٓا۟ إِلَى ٱللَّهِ ۖ إِنِّى لَكُم مِّنْهُ نَذِيرٌ مُّبِينٌ (٥٠)
‘So flee toward Allah. Indeed, I am from Him a manifest warner to you. (50)
وَلَا تَجْعَلُوا۟ مَعَ ٱللَّهِ إِلَـٰهًا ءَاخَرَ ۖ إِنِّى لَكُم مِّنْهُ نَذِيرٌ مُّبِينٌ (٥١)
Do not set up another god besides Allah. Indeed I am from Him a manifest warner to you.’ (51)

(Commentary)

The verses point to several of the signs (آيات) of Allah that demonstrate His oneness in Lordship (الربوبية) — the absolute authority of Allah in creating, sustaining, and governing all existence — and the fact that the management (التدبير) of the earth, the heavens, humanity, and their provisions returns to Him. A necessary consequence of this is the possibility, indeed the necessity, of the descent of divine religion through the medium of messengership. It further necessitates the truthfulness of the prophetic call regarding the promise of resurrection and recompense: "Indeed, what you are promised is true, and indeed the Judgment is sure to occur." An indication of the specific method this Surah employs in its argumentation was provided in the previous commentary.

His saying, the Exalted: وَفِي الْأَرْضِ آياتٌ لِلْمُوقِنِينَ — "In the earth are signs for those who have conviction" (51:20). The conclusion that follows at the end of these verses in His saying: "So flee to Allah... and do not set up another god with Allah," testifies that the aim of these verses and proofs is to establish His oneness in Lordship, not merely to prove the origin of His existence or that creation terminates in Him, and the like.

The verse contains an allusion to the wondrous signs within the earth that point to the unity of management (التدبير) arising from the oneness of its Manager (المدبر). This includes the land and sea, mountains and hills, springs and rivers, minerals and their interconnected benefits, all harmonized with one another. The plants and animals upon the earth benefit from them within a single, continuous system devoid of coincidence or chance. Upon these signs are manifest the traces of power (القدرة), knowledge (العلم), and wisdom (الحكم), indicating that their creation and the management of their affairs terminate in a Creator, Manager, Powerful, Knowing, and Wise.

Whichever aspect of the earth one intends to examine, and whichever direction of the general management (التدبير العام) operating within it one turns toward, it serves as a clear sign and a radiant proof of the oneness of its Lord, who has no partner. In it, the truth is unveiled for the people of certainty; thus, in it are signs for those who have conviction (الموقنين).

His saying, the Exalted: وَفِي أَنْفُسِكُمْ أَفَلا تُبْصِرُونَ — "and in your own souls [as well]. Will you not then perceive?" (51:21) is coordinated with His saying: "In the earth." That is: and in your own souls are manifest signs for whoever looks into them and focuses their gaze upon them; will you not then perceive?

The signs within the souls (النفوس) include the composition of bodies from their limbs, and the components of those limbs, down to the simple elements (البسائط), along with their wondrous actions and effects which are unified despite their multiplicity, all managed by a single Manager. They also include the various states that befall the body, such as the embryonic stage, childhood, adolescence, youth, and old age.

Other signs pertain to the attachment of the souls—meaning the spirits (الأرواح)—to the bodies, such as the senses of sight, hearing, taste, smell, and touch. These are the primary pathways for the souls to perceive the external world, thereby distinguishing good from evil and the beneficial from the harmful, so that the soul may strive toward its perfection and flee from what is incompatible with it. In each of these is a vast, functioning system, essentially distinct from the others; sight, for instance, has no knowledge of what hearing performs within its own system, and so on. Yet, despite this separation and distinctness, they are all harmonized, working under the management of a single manager, which is the governing soul (النفس المدبرة), and Allah encompasses them from beyond.

Of this nature are the other faculties emanating from the souls into the bodies, such as the faculty of anger (القوة الغضبية) and the faculty of desire (القوة الشهوية), along with their appendages and branches. Despite the distinctness of each faculty from the others and the separation of the system operating within each, they all fall under the management of a single manager; all their branches cooperate and harmonize to serve it.

The system of management (نظام التدبير) belonging to each of these managed entities was found for it at the moment it came into existence, appearing from the very beginning without any interval. It is not something produced by its own choice, nor did it bring it into being for itself through thought or reflection. Thus, the system of its management, like the soul itself, is from a Maker who fashioned it and bound it to its system through His management.

Among these are the spiritual signs (الآيات الروحانية) occurring in the realm of the souls, manifest to those who return to their inner selves and observe Allah, the Glorified, therein. These are among the signs of Allah that the descriptions of describers cannot encompass; through them, the door of certainty (اليقين) is opened, and the observer is ranked among the people of conviction (الموقنين), seeing the kingdom (الملكوت) — the inner, celestial reality and sovereign dominion — of the heavens and the earth, as the Exalted said: وَكَذلِكَ نُرِي إِبْراهِيمَ مَلَكُوتَ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ وَلِيَكُونَ مِنَ الْمُوقِنِينَ — "Thus did We show Abraham the dominion of the heavens and the earth, that he might be of those who possess conviction" (6:75).

His saying, the Exalted: وَفِي السَّماءِ رِزْقُكُمْ وَما تُوعَدُونَ — "And in the heaven is your provision and what you are promised" (51:22). It has been said that "the heaven" (السماء) refers to the direction of height, for everything that is above you and shades you is linguistically a "heaven." "Provision" (الرزق) refers to the rain that Allah sends down upon the earth, by which He brings forth the various types of things people eat, wear, and benefit from. The Exalted has said: وَما أَنْزَلَ اللهُ مِنَ السَّماءِ مِنْ رِزْقٍ فَأَحْيا بِهِ الْأَرْضَ بعد مَوْتِها — "and the provision that Allah sends down from the sky, with which He revives the earth after its death" (45:5). Thus, rain is called provision; so what is meant by provision is its cause, or there is an implied possessive, i.e., "the cause of your provision."

It has also been said that it refers to the celestial causes of provision, such as the sun, the moon, the stars, and the variation of the rising and setting points that delineate the four seasons and the succession of night and day—all of which are causes of provision. Thus, the speech implies a possessive, i.e., "the causes of your provision," or it is a metaphor (تجوز) based on the claim that the existence of the causes therein is the existence of the things caused.

It has also been said that the provision being "in the heaven" means its decree (تقديره) is there, or that provisions are written in the Preserved Tablet (اللوح المحفوظ) therein.

It is possible that it refers to the realm of the Unseen (عالم الغيب) — that which lies beyond sensory perception and empirical knowledge — for all things, including provisions, descend from Allah, the Glorified. He has explicitly stated this regarding certain things, such as His saying: وَأَنْزَلَ لَكُمْ مِنَ الْأَنْعامِ ثَمانِيَةَ أَزْواجٍ — "and He sent down for you eight mates of the cattle" (39:6), and His saying: وَأَنْزَلْنَا الْحَدِيدَ فِيهِ بَأْسٌ شَدِيدٌ — "and We sent down iron, wherein is great might" (57:25), and His saying in a general manner: وَإِنْ مِنْ شَيْءٍ إِلَّا عِنْدَنا خَزائِنُهُ وَما نُنَزِّلُهُ إِلَّا بِقَدَرٍ مَعْلُومٍ — "There is not a thing but its treasures are with Us, and We do not send it down except in a known measure" (15:21). Provision (الرزق) refers to everything a human benefits from for their survival, including food, drink, clothing, housing, marriage, children, knowledge, strength, and other things.

His saying: وَما تُوعَدُونَ — "and what you are promised" is coordinated with "your provision." It appears that it refers to Paradise, due to His saying: عِنْدَها جَنَّةُ الْمَأْوى — "near which is the Garden of the Abode" (53:15). The view of some that it refers to Paradise and Hell, or reward and punishment, is not compatible with His saying: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ كَذَّبُوا بِآياتِنا وَاسْتَكْبَرُوا عَنْها لا تُفَتَّحُ لَهُمْ أَبْوابُ السَّماءِ وَلا يَدْخُلُونَ الْجَنَّةَ حَتَّى يَلِجَ الْجَمَلُ فِي سَمِّ الْخِياطِ — "Indeed those who deny Our signs and are disdainful of them, the gates of the heaven will not be opened for them, nor will they enter paradise until the camel passes through the needle’s eye" (7:40).

However, the attribution of the descent of worldly punishment to the heaven is repeated in the Quran, such as His saying: فأَنْزَلْنا عَلَى الَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا رِجْزاً مِنَ السَّماءِ — "so We sent down upon the wrongdoers a plague from the sky" (2:59), and others.

It is narrated from some that His saying: "and what you are promised" is a subject (مبتدأ) whose predicate (خبر) is His saying: "By the Lord of the heaven and the earth, it is indeed the truth," and the "and" (الواو) is for commencement (الاستئناف); but this is a meaning far from the [natural] understanding.

His saying, the Exalted: فَوَ رَبِّ السَّماءِ وَالْأَرْضِ إِنَّهُ لَحَقٌّ مِثْلَ ما أَنَّكُمْ تَنْطِقُونَ — "By the Lord of the heaven and the earth, it is indeed as real as your speaking" (51:23). Speech (النطق) is talking. The pronoun in "it is indeed" (إنه) refers to what was mentioned regarding provision and what you are promised being in the heaven. The "truth" (الحق) is that which is established and mandated in the divine decree (القضاء الإلهي), rather than being a secondary or coincidental matter.

The meaning is: I swear by the Lord of the heaven and the earth that what We have mentioned—of your provision and what you are promised of Paradise (which is also part of provision, as the Quran repeatedly calls Paradise "provision," such as His saying: لَهُمْ مَغْفِرَةٌ وَرِزْقٌ كَرِيمٌ — "for them is forgiveness and a noble provision" (8:74), and others)—being in the heaven is as established and decreed as your own speaking and talking, which is a reality you do not doubt.

Some have allowed that the pronoun in "it is indeed" (إنه) refers only to "what you are promised," or only to "provision," or to Allah, or to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), or to the Quran, or to the religion in His saying: "and indeed the Judgment is sure to occur," or to the Day in His saying: "When is the Day of Judgment?", or to everything that preceded from the beginning of the Surah to this point. Perhaps the most appropriate view is that it refers to what was mentioned in His saying: "And in the heaven is your provision and what you are promised," as we previously stated.

A Discourse on the Correspondence between Provision and the Provided-for

Provision (الرزق) — that which sustains and benefits a being — in the sense of that which is consumed, is that which aids another thing in its survival by joining with it or attaching to it in any sense. Examples include food, which aids a human in their life and survival by becoming part of their body, or a spouse, who aids their partner in satisfying instinct and preserving progeny, and so on.

It is evident that material things provide for one another, such as humans being provided for by animals and plants. Just as the stages of being and various states that attach to the provided-for (المرزوق) in its survival are stages of existence (الكون) attached to it and attributed to it, they are identically stages of existence attached to the provision (الرزق) and attributed to it, even if the names sometimes change. Just as a human, through nourishment, acquires new parts in their body, the food becomes a new part of their body called such-and-forth.

It is also evident that the Decree (القضاء) encompasses existence and pervades all things; by it, whatever occurs to every thing in itself and its stages of existence is determined. In other words, the chain of events, with its functioning system, is composed of complete causes (علل تامة) and necessary effects (معلولات ضرورية).

From this, it becomes clear that provision (الرزق) and the provided-for (المرزوق) are inseparable (متلازمان) and do not part. There is no meaning to an existent undergoing a new stage in its existence through the joining or attachment of something, except with the existence of that joined or attached thing which shares that stage with it. Thus, there is no meaning to a provided-for being aided in its survival without provision, nor is there meaning to a realized provision without a provided-for. Similarly, there is no meaning to provision exceeding what the provided-for requires, nor to the survival of a provided-for without provision. Therefore, provision enters into the divine decree (القضاء الإلهي) primarily, not accidentally or secondarily; this is the meaning of provision being "truth" (حق).

His saying, the Exalted: هَلْ أَتاكَ حَدِيثُ ضَيْفِ إِبْراهِيمَ الْمُكْرَمِينَ — "Did the story of Abraham’s honored guests reach you?" (51:24) is an allusion to the story of the honored angels entering upon Abraham (peace be upon him) and their bringing glad tidings to him and his wife, and then their destruction of the people of Lot. In it is a sign of the oneness of Lordship, as previously indicated.

In His saying: "Did there reach you the story," there is an aggrandizement (تفخيم) of the matter of the story. "The honored" (الْمُكْرَمِينَ)—who are the angels that entered upon Abraham—is an adjective for "guest" (ضيف); it is singular because it is originally a verbal noun (مصدر) that is not dualized or pluralized.

His saying, the Exalted: إِذْ دَخَلُوا عَلَيْهِ فَقالُوا سَلاماً قالَ سَلامٌ قَوْمٌ مُنْكَرُونَ — "When they entered upon him and said, ‘Peace!’ He said, ‘Peace! [You are] a people unknown [to me].’" (51:25). The adverbial phrase (إذ) relates to "story" (حديث) in the previous verse. "Peace" (سلاماً) is the object of the speech, and its governing verb is omitted, i.e., they said: "We greet you with peace."

His saying: "He said, ‘Peace!’" is the speech and its object. "Peace" (سلامٌ) is a subject (مبتدأ) whose predicate is omitted, the estimation being "Peace be upon you." By providing the response as a nominal sentence (جملة اسمية) indicating stability, his (peace be upon him) greeting was better than theirs ("Peace!"), which was a verbal sentence indicating occurrence.

His saying: "a people unknown" (قَوْمٌ مُنْكَرُونَ) is apparently a narration of what Abraham said to himself. Its meaning is that when he saw them, he found them strange and said to himself that these are an unknown people. This does not contradict what occurs in His saying: فَلَمَّا رَأى أَيْدِيَهُمْ لا تَصِلُ إِلَيْهِ نَكِرَهُمْ — "But when he saw their hands not reaching for it, he took them for strangers" (11:70), where his finding them strange is mentioned after bringing the roasted calf to them. For what is in this Surah is his internal thought, while what is in Surah Hud is its appearance on his face such that it was observed from him.

This meaning is more appropriate than the view of a group of commentators that it is a narration of his (peace be upon him) saying to them, the estimation being: "You are an unknown people."

His saying, the Exalted: فَراغَ إِلى أَهْلِهِ فَجاءَ بِعِجْلٍ سَمِينٍ — "Then he withdrew to his family and brought a fat calf" (51:26). Withdrawing (الروغ) is going by way of a ruse, as al-Raghib said; others said it is going to something in secret. The first meaning returns to the second.

What is meant by the "fat calf" is a roasted one, evidenced by His saying: "Then he placed it before them," or the "then" (الفاء) is explanatory (فصيحة), the estimation being: "He brought a fat calf, slaughtered it, roasted it, and placed it before them."

His saying, the Exalted: فَقَرَّبَهُ إِلَيْهِمْ قالَ أَلا تَأْكُلُونَ — "and placed it before them. He said, ‘Will you not eat?’" (51:27). He offered the food to the angels while he thought they were human.

His saying, the Exalted: فَأَوْجَسَ مِنْهُمْ خِيفَةً قالُوا لا تَخَفْ — "Then he conceived a fear of them. They said, ‘Do not be afraid!’" (51:28). The "then" (الفاء) is explanatory, the estimation being: "They did not stretch their hands toward it, so when he saw that, he found them strange and conceived a fear of them." Conceiving (الإيجاس) is a feeling within the heart, and fear (الخيفة) is a specific type of fear; i.e., he harbored within himself a type of fear of them.

His saying: "They said, ‘Do not be afraid!’" is presented without a conjunction (فصل) because it is in the sense of a response to an implied question, as if it were asked: "What happened after he conceived the fear?" and it was said: "They said: Do not be afraid, and they gave him the good news of a wise son." Thus, they replaced his fear with security and joy. What is meant by "a wise son" is either Ishmael or Isaac, and the disagreement regarding this has been previously mentioned.

His saying, the Exalted: فَأَقْبَلَتِ امْرَأَتُهُ فِي صَرَّةٍ فَصكَّتْ وَجْهَها وَقالَتْ عَجُوزٌ عَقِيمٌ — "Then his wife came forward with a cry and struck her face, and said, ‘[I am] a barren old woman!’" (51:29). In al-Majma': The cry (الصرة) is an intense shout, derived from the creaking (صرير) of a door; a group is also called a sarrah. He said: Striking (الصك) is hitting with heavy force.

The meaning is: Abraham's (peace be upon him) wife came forward—when she heard the glad tidings—in a clamor and shouting, slapped her face, and said: "I am a barren old woman, so how shall I give birth?" Or the meaning is: "Does a barren old woman give birth to a son?" It has been said that sarrah means a group, and that she came to them in a group and struck her face and said what she said; but the first meaning is more in accord with the context.

His saying, the Exalted: قالُوا كَذلِكَ قالَ رَبُّكِ إِنَّهُ هُوَ الْحَكِيمُ الْعَلِيمُ — "They said, ‘So has your Lord said. Indeed He is the All-wise, the All-knowing.’" (51:30). The "so" (كذلك) refers to what they gave her glad tidings of, despite her and her husband's current state—she being a barren old woman and her husband an old man touched by senility. For her Lord is Wise (حكيم); He does not intend what He intends except by His wisdom. He is Knowing (عليم); no aspect of the matter is hidden from Him.

His saying, the Exalted: قالَ فَما خَطْبُكُمْ أَيُّهَا الْمُرْسَلُونَ — "He said, ‘What is your errand, O messengers?’" (51:31) to His saying: لِلْمُسْرِفِينَ — "for the profligate" (51:34). The errand (الخطب) is a grave and important matter. "Stones of clay" are petrified clay. Marking (التسويم) is to mark something, meaning to make it possess a sign (سومة).

The meaning is: Abraham (peace be upon him) "said": "What is your errand" and the grave matter you have, "O messengers" from among the angels? "They said"—meaning the angels to Abraham—"Indeed we have been sent to a guilty people," who are the people of Lot, "to let loose upon them stones of clay"—petrified clay which Allah called sijjil—"marked" with a sign "with your Lord for the profligate," specifically for them to destroy them. It appears that the "the" (اللام) in "the profligate" (المسرفين) is for specific reference (العهد).

His saying, the Exalted: فَأَخْرَجْنا مَنْ كانَ فِيها مِنَ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ — "So We brought out those of the faithful who were in it" (51:35) to His saying: الْعَذابَ الْأَلِيمَ — "the painful punishment" (51:37). The "then" (الفاء) is explanatory, and the story has been summarized by omitting the angels' journey to Lot, their arrival to him, and the people's intent toward them, until they brought out the family of Lot from the town. The story is detailed in other places of His speech.

His saying: "So We brought out..." is an explanation of their destruction along with its preamble. The pronoun in "in it" (فيها) refers to the town understood from the context. "A house of the Muslims" (بَيْتٍ مِنَ الْمُسْلِمِينَ) is the house of Lot. His saying: "And We left in it a sign" refers to their destruction and making their land upside down. "Leaving" (الترك) here is a metaphor for preservation. These specifics have been explained in other parts of His speech.

The meaning is: When they went to Lot and what happened of their affair occurred, "We brought out those who were in it"—in the town—"of the believers, but We did not find in it other than one house of the Muslims," who were the family of Lot. "And We left in it"—in their land, by its overturning and their destruction—"a sign" pointing to Our Lordship and the vanity of partners, "for those who fear the painful punishment" among mankind.

His saying, the Exalted: وَفِي مُوسى إِذْ أَرْسَلْناهُ إِلى فِرْعَوْنَ بِسُلْطانٍ مُبِينٍ — "And in Moses [too], when We sent him to Pharaoh with a manifest authority" (51:38) is coordinated with His saying: "And We left in it a sign," the estimation being: "And in Moses is a sign." What is meant by "manifest authority" (سلطان مبين) are the dazzling proofs that were with him of miraculous signs.

His saying, the Exalted: فَتَوَلَّى بِرُكْنِهِ وَقالَ ساحِرٌ أَوْ مَجْنُونٌ — "But he turned away with his bulwark, and said, ‘[He is] a magician or a madman!’" (51:39). Turning away (التولي) is averting oneself. The "with" (الباء) in "with his bulwark" (بركنه) is for accompaniment (المصاحبة); his "bulwark" refers to his soldiers, as supported by the following verse. The meaning is: he turned away along with his soldiers. It has also been said that the "with" is for transitivity (التعدية), meaning: he made his bulwark turn away and avert themselves.

His saying: "and said, ‘[He is] a magician or a madman!’" means he said at one time he is a madman, as in His saying: إِنَّ رَسُولَكُمُ الَّذِي أُرْسِلَ إِلَيْكُمْ لَمَجْنُونٌ — "Indeed your messenger who has been sent to you is surely crazy" (26:27), and at another time he said: he is a magician, as in His saying: إِنَّ هذا لَساحِرٌ عَلِيمٌ — "This is indeed a knowing magician" (26:34).

His saying, the Exalted: فَأَخَذْناهُ وَجُنُودَهُ فَنَبَذْناهُمْ فِي الْيَمِّ وَهُوَ مُلِيمٌ — "So We seized him and his hosts and cast them into the sea, while he was to blame" (51:40). Casting (النبذ) is throwing something without giving it any weight. The sea (اليم) is the ocean. To blame (المليم) is one who does that for which he is blamed, from alama, meaning he did something blameworthy, just as aghraba means he did something strange.

The meaning is: We seized him and his soldiers—who were his bulwark—and threw them into the sea while he had committed such disbelief, denial, and tyranny for which he is blamed. Pharaoh was specifically mentioned with blame, even though all shared in it, because he was their leader who led them to destruction. The Exalted said: يَقْدُمُ قَوْمَهُ يَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ فَأَوْرَدَهُمُ النَّارَ — "He will lead his people on the Day of Resurrection and conduct them into the Fire" (11:98).

In the speech, there is an allusion to the greatness of power and the terror of the seizure, which is that the matter of Pharaoh and his soldiers is not hidden.

His saying, the Exalted: وَفِي عادٍ إِذْ أَرْسَلْنا عَلَيْهِمُ الرِّيحَ الْعَقِيمَ — "And in ‘Ad [too], when We unleashed against them the barren wind" (51:41) is coordinated with what preceded it; i.e., and in 'Ad also is a sign when We sent against them—meaning We unleashed upon them—the barren wind.

The barren wind (الريح العقيم) is the wind that has become sterile and prevented from producing any desired benefit of the winds, such as forming clouds, pollinating trees, winnowing food, benefiting animals, or purifying the air, as has been said. Rather, its only effect is destruction, as the following verse indicates.

His saying, the Exalted: ما تَذَرُ مِنْ شَيْءٍ أَتَتْ عَلَيْهِ إِلَّا جَعَلَتْهُ كَالرَّمِيمِ — "It spared nothing that it came upon, but made it like decayed bones" (51:42). "It spared nothing" (ما تَذَرُ) means it left nothing. Decayed bones (الرميم) refers to something destroyed and worn out, like crushed, ancient bone. The meaning is clear.

His saying, the Exalted: وَفِي ثَمُودَ إِذْ قِيلَ لَهُمْ تَمَتَّعُوا حَتَّى حِينٍ — "And in Thamud [too], when they were told, ‘Enjoy yourselves for a while’" (51:43) to His saying: مُنْتَصِرِينَ — "capable of helping themselves" (51:45) is coordinated with what preceded it; i.e., and in Thamud also is a sign when it was said to them: "Enjoy yourselves for a while." The speaker was their prophet Salih (peace be upon him) when he said to them: تَمَتَّعُوا فِي دارِكُمْ ثَلاثَةَ أَيَّامٍ ذلِكَ وَعْدٌ غَيْرُ مَكْذُوبٍ — "Enjoy yourselves in your homes for three days: that is a promise not to be belied" (11:65). He said that to them when they hamstrung the she-camel, so he gave them a respite of three days to return from their disbelief and defiance, but it did not benefit them, and the word of punishment became due against them.

His saying: فَعَتَوْا عَنْ أَمْرِ رَبِّهِمْ فَأَخَذَتْهُمُ الصَّاعِقَةُ وَهُمْ يَنْظُرُونَ — "But they defied the command of their Lord; so the thunderbolt seized them while they looked on" (51:44). Defiance (العتو)—according to what al-Raghib mentioned—is recoiling from obedience, thus it applies to rebellion. What is meant by this defiance is the defiance against the command to return to Allah during the days of respite. Thus, one should not find it problematic that their defiance of Allah's command preceded their enjoyment—as appears from the details of the story—while the verse indicates the opposite.

His saying: "so the thunderbolt seized them while they looked on" does not contradict what is mentioned in another place regarding the "cry" (الصيحة) instead of the "thunderbolt," such as His saying: وَأَخَذَ الَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا الصَّيْحَةُ — "and the Cry seized those who were wrongdoers" (11:67), for it is possible that both occurred together in their punishment.

His saying: فَمَا اسْتَطاعُوا مِنْ قِيامٍ وَما كانُوا مُنْتَصِرِينَ — "They could neither stand up, nor were they capable of helping themselves" (51:45). It is not unlikely that "they could" (اسْتَطاعُوا) implies the meaning of being able to manage, and "stand up" (مِنْ قِيامٍ) is its object; i.e., they were not able to manage to stand up from their places to flee from Allah's punishment. This is a metaphor for the fact that they were not given respite even for the duration it takes to stand up from one's seat.

His saying: "nor were they capable of helping themselves" is coordinated with "they could neither"; i.e., they were not helped by the victory of others to repel the punishment from themselves. The sum of the two sentences is that they were unable to repel the punishment from themselves, neither by their own power nor by a helper who might assist them.

His saying, the Exalted: وَقَوْمَ نُوحٍ مِنْ قَبْلُ إِنَّهُمْ كانُوا قَوْماً فاسِقِينَ — "And the people of Noah before [them]; indeed they were a transgressing lot" (51:46) is coordinated with the previous stories. "The people of Noah" (قَوْمَ نُوحٍ) is in the accusative case due to a deleted verb, the estimation being: "And We destroyed the people of Noah before 'Ad and Thamud; indeed they were transgressing against the command of Allah."

Thus, there is a command and a prohibition with which people were charged by Allah, the Glorified, who is their Lord and the Lord of all things. He called them to the true religion through the tongues of His messengers. Therefore, what the prophets (peace be upon them) brought is truth from Allah, and among what they brought is the promise of resurrection and recompense.

His saying, the Exalted: وَالسَّماءَ بَنَيْناها بِأَيْدٍ وَإِنَّا لَمُوسِعُونَ — "We built the sky with might, and We are indeed expanding [it]" (51:47) is a return to the previous context in His saying: "In the earth are signs for those who have conviction," etc. Might (الأيد) refers to power and blessing. According to each of the two meanings, what is appropriate for His saying: "and We are indeed expanding" (وَإِنَّا لَمُوسِعُونَ) is determined.

According to the first meaning: "And the heaven We built with a power whose extent cannot be described, and We are indeed possessors of vastness in power that nothing can frustrate." According to the second: "And the heaven We built accompanied by a blessing that cannot be measured, and We are indeed possessors of vastness and wealth; Our treasures are not exhausted by giving and providing. We provide from the heaven for whom We will, so We expand provision as We will."

It is also possible that "expanding" (موسعون) is from "spending expansively," i.e., increasing it; thus, the meaning would be the expansion of the creation of the heaven, as modern mathematical research tends toward today.

His saying, the Exalted: وَالْأَرْضَ فَرَشْناها فَنِعْمَ الْماهِدُونَ — "And the earth We have spread it out; and how excellent spreaders We are!" (51:48). Spreading (الفرش) is to extend, as is cradling (المهد); i.e., and the earth We have extended and leveled so that you may settle upon it and inhabit it; how excellent are We as extenders. This spreading and leveling does not contradict the earth's sphericity.

His saying, the Exalted: وَمِنْ كُلِّ شَيْءٍ خَلَقْنا زَوْجَيْنِ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَذَكَّرُونَ — "In all things We have created pairs so that you may take admonition" (51:49). Pairs (الزوجان) are two opposites, one of which is completed by the other: an agent and a recipient, like male and female. It has been said that it refers to all opposites, such as male and female, heaven and earth, night and day, land and sea, humans and jinn. It has also been said it refers specifically to male and female.

His saying: "so that you may take admonition" (لَعَلَّكُمْ تَذَكَّرُونَ) means: so that you may remember that their Creator is transcendent above having a pair or a partner; He is One, the Unique.

His saying, the Exalted: فَفِرُّوا إِلَى اللهِ إِنِّي لَكُمْ مِنْهُ نَذِيرٌ مُبِينٌ وَلا تَجْعَلُوا مَعَ اللهِ إِلهاً آخَرَ إِنِّي لَكُمْ مِنْهُ نَذِيرٌ مُبِينٌ — "So flee to Allah. Indeed I am a manifest warner to you from Him. And do not set up another god with Allah. Indeed I am a manifest warner to you from Him" (51:50-51). In these two verses is a derivation from the preceding proofs for His oneness in Lordship and Divinity (الألوهية). They contain stories of several past nations who disbelieved in Allah and His messengers, which led them to the punishment of extirpation.

Thus, what is meant by "fleeing to Allah" is to devote oneself to Him, turning away from disbelief and the punishment it entails, by believing in Him, the Exalted, alone and taking Him as a worshipped God with no partner.

His saying: "And do not set up another god with Allah" is like an explanation for His saying: "So flee to Allah"; i.e., what is meant by believing in Him is believing in Him alone, with no partner in Divinity and worship.

He repeated His saying: "Indeed I am a manifest warner to you from Him" to emphasize the warning. The two verses are narrated from the tongue of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم).

(Narrative Discussion)

In the Tafsir of al-Qummi, regarding His saying, the Exalted: وَفِي أَنْفُسِكُمْ أَفَلا تُبْصِرُونَ — "and in your own souls [as well]. Will you not then perceive?" (51:21), he said: "He created you hearing and seeing; you become angry at times and pleased at times, you become hungry at times and full at times—all of that is from the signs of Allah."

I say: And in al-Majma', it is attributed to Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him).

In al-Tawhid, with its chain of transmission to Hisham ibn Salim, he said: "Abu 'Abdillah (peace be upon him) was asked: 'By what did you know your Lord?' He replied: 'By the dissolving of resolve and the breaking of intent. I resolved, and my resolve was dissolved; I intended, and my intent was broken.'"

I say: It is also narrated in al-Khisal from him, from his father, from his grandfather, from the Commander of the Faithful (peace be upon him).

In al-Durr al-Manthur, al-Khara'iti narrated in Masawi al-Akhlaq from 'Ali ibn Abi Talib regarding وَفِي أَنْفُسِكُمْ أَفَلا تُبْصِرُونَ — "and in your own souls [as well]. Will you not then perceive?" (51:21), he said: "The passage of excrement and urine."

I say: This narration, like the two preceding ones, is intended to explain some instances of the ways of knowledge.

In the same source, Ibn al-Naqur and al-Daylami narrated from 'Ali, from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) regarding His saying: وَفِي السَّماءِ رِزْقُكُمْ وَما تُوعَدُونَ — "And in the heaven is your provision and what you are promised" (51:22), he said: "The rain."

I say: Al-Qummi narrated something similar in his Tafsir, as a disconnected (مرسلاً) and anonymous (مضمراً) report.

In the Irshad of al-Mufid, from 'Ali (peace be upon him) in a hadith: "Seek provision, for it is guaranteed for its seeker."

In al-Tawhid, with its chain of transmission to Abu al-Bakhtari, he said: "Ja'far ibn Muhammad narrated to me from his father, from his grandfather, from 'Ali ibn Abi Talib (peace be upon him), from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) that he said: 'O 'Ali: Indeed, certainty (اليقين) is that you do not please anyone at the cost of Allah's displeasure, nor praise anyone for what Allah has given you, nor blame anyone for what Allah has not given you—for provision is not brought by the greed of the greedy, nor turned away by the dislike of the disliker...'" [the hadith continues].

In al-Majma': فَأَقْبَلَتِ امْرَأَتُهُ فِي صَرَّةٍ — "Then his wife came forward with a cry" (51:29). It is said: "in a group." Narrated from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him).

In al-Durr al-Manthur, al-Faryabi and Ibn al-Mundhir narrated from 'Ali ibn Abi Talib, who said: "The barren wind (الريح العقيم) is the nakba' (the wind that deviates from the four cardinal directions)."

In al-Tawhid, with its chain of transmission to Muhammad ibn Muslim, he said: "I asked Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him) and said: 'The saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: يا إِبْلِيسُ ما مَنَعَكَ أَنْ تَسْجُدَ لِما خَلَقْتُ بِيَدَيَ — "O Iblis! What kept you from prostrating before that which I have created with My own two hands?" (38:75)?' He replied: 'The hand (اليد) in the speech of the Arabs refers to power (القوة) and blessing (النعمة). Allah said: وَاذْكُرْ عَبْدَنا داوُدَ ذَا الْأَيْدِ — "And remember Our servant David, the man of might" (38:17), and He said: وَالسَّماءَ بَنَيْناها بِأَيْدٍ — "We built the sky with might" (51:47), meaning with power. And He said: وَأَيَّدَهُمْ بِرُوحٍ مِنْهُ — "and He strengthened them with a spirit from Him" (58:22), meaning with power. And it is said: "So-and-so has a white hand (يد بيضاء) with me," meaning a blessing.'"

In al-Tawhid, with its chain of transmission to Abu al-Hasan al-Rida (peace be upon him), in a long sermon, in which he said: "...By His endowing the senses with their sensory nature (تشعيره المشاعر), it is known that He has no sense; by His making the substances substantial (تجهيره الجواهر), it is known that He has no substance; by His creating opposition between things, it is known that He has no opposite; and by His creating companionship between things, it is known that He has no companion. He opposed light with darkness, dryness with moisture, roughness with softness, and cold with heat, harmonizing their mutual enemies and separating their mutual close ones. Their separation points to their Separator, and their harmony points to their Harmonizer; and that is His saying: مِنْ كُلِّ شَيْءٍ خَلَقْنا زَوْجَيْنِ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَذَكَّرُونَ — "In all things We have created pairs so that you may take admonition" (51:49).

He separated between "before" and "after"—so that it may be known that He has no "before" and no "after." They testify by their innate dispositions (غرائزها) that their Planter (مغرزها) has no innate disposition; they inform by their temporality (توقيتها) that their Timer (موقتها) has no time. He veiled some of them from others—so that it may be known that there is no veil between Him and His creation."

In al-Majma', regarding His saying, the Exalted: فَفِرُّوا إِلَى اللهِ — "So flee to Allah" (51:50). It is said: "Its meaning is: perform the Hajj." Narrated from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him).

I say: It is narrated in al-Kafi and in al-Ma'ani, with a chain of transmission from Abu al-Jarud from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him); perhaps it is by way of application (التطبيق).

Ayahs

كَذَٰلِكَ مَآ أَتَى ٱلَّذِينَ مِن قَبْلِهِم مِّن رَّسُولٍ إِلَّا قَالُوا۟ سَاحِرٌ أَوْ مَجْنُونٌ (٥٢)
So it was that there did not come any apostle to those who were before them but they said, ‘A magician,’ or ‘A crazy man!’ (52)
أَتَوَاصَوْا۟ بِهِۦ ۚ بَلْ هُمْ قَوْمٌ طَاغُونَ (٥٣)
Did they enjoin this upon one another?! Rather, they were a rebellious lot. (53)
فَتَوَلَّ عَنْهُمْ فَمَآ أَنتَ بِمَلُومٍ (٥٤)
So turn away from them, as you will not be blameworthy. (54)
وَذَكِّرْ فَإِنَّ ٱلذِّكْرَىٰ تَنفَعُ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ (٥٥)
And admonish, for admonition indeed benefits the faithful. (55)
وَمَا خَلَقْتُ ٱلْجِنَّ وَٱلْإِنسَ إِلَّا لِيَعْبُدُونِ (٥٦)
I did not create the jinn and the humans except that they may worship Me. (56)
مَآ أُرِيدُ مِنْهُم مِّن رِّزْقٍ وَمَآ أُرِيدُ أَن يُطْعِمُونِ (٥٧)
I desire no provision from them, nor do I desire that they should feed Me. (57)
إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ هُوَ ٱلرَّزَّاقُ ذُو ٱلْقُوَّةِ ٱلْمَتِينُ (٥٨)
Indeed it is Allah who is the All-provider, Powerful and All-strong. (58)
فَإِنَّ لِلَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا۟ ذَنُوبًا مِّثْلَ ذَنُوبِ أَصْحَـٰبِهِمْ فَلَا يَسْتَعْجِلُونِ (٥٩)
Indeed the lot of those who do wrong [now] will be like the lot of their [earlier] counterparts. So let them not ask Me to hasten on [that fate]. (59)
فَوَيْلٌ لِّلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ مِن يَوْمِهِمُ ٱلَّذِى يُوعَدُونَ (٦٠)
Woe to the faithless for the day they are promised! (60)

(Commentary)

This is the conclusion of the Surah, in which the discourse returns to what was mentioned at its opening regarding their denial of the promised Resurrection and their confronting the Message with discordant speech, followed by a warning of the Promised Day.

His saying, the Exalted: كَذلِكَ ما أَتَى الَّذِينَ مِنْ قَبْلِهِمْ مِنْ رَسُولٍ إِلَّا قالُوا ساحِرٌ أَوْ مَجْنُونٌ "So it is: there did not come any apostle to those who were before them but they said, ‘A magician,’ or ‘A crazy man.’" (51:52). That is, the matter is as such. Thus, His saying: "So it is (كَذلِكَ)" serves as a summary of what preceded regarding their denial and their discordance in speech.

His saying: "there did not come any apostle to those who were before them (ما أَتَى الَّذِينَ مِنْ قَبْلِهِمْ...)" etc., is an explanation of the likeness being drawn.

His saying, the Exalted: أَتَواصَوْا بِهِ بَلْ هُمْ قَوْمٌ طاغُونَ "Have they handed down this legacy to one another? Rather they are a rebellious people." (51:53). Mutual legacy (التواصي) — the act of people enjoining one another with a matter. The pronoun in "to it (بِهِ)" refers to the [discordant] speech. The interrogative is for expressing wonder. The meaning is: Did these nations enjoin one another—did the predecessor enjoin the successor—upon this speech? No, rather they are a rebellious (طاغون) people whose rebellion (طغيان) incites them to this speech.

His saying, the Exalted: فَتَوَلَّ عَنْهُمْ فَما أَنْتَ بِمَلُومٍ "So disregard them, for you will not be blamed." (51:54). This is a derivation from their rebellion, arrogance, and persistence in obstinacy and stubbornness. The meaning is: Since the situation is such, and they have not responded to you except with the likes of their saying "magician" or "crazy man," and your call has only increased them in obstinacy, then turn away from them and do not argue with them over the truth, for you are not to be blamed (بِمَلُومٍ); you have shown the path (المحجة) and completed the proof (الحجة).

His saying, the Exalted: وَذَكِّرْ فَإِنَّ الذِّكْرى تَنْفَعُ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ "And admonish, for admonition indeed benefits the faithful." (51:55). This is a derivation from the command to turn away from them; it is a command to remind after the prohibition of arguing with them. The meaning is: Continue with the reminder and exhortation; remind as you used to remind, for the reminder benefits the believers, unlike disputation and arguing with those rebellious ones, for it does not benefit them at all and only increases them in rebellion and disbelief.

His saying, the Exalted: وَما خَلَقْتُ الْجِنَّ وَالْإِنْسَ إِلَّا لِيَعْبُدُونِ "I did not create the jinn and the humans except that they may worship Me." (51:56). In this verse, there is a transition (التفات) from the context of speaking in the plural (تكلم بالغير) to speaking in the singular (تكلم وحده). This is because the previously mentioned acts attributed to Him, the Exalted—such as creation, sending messengers, and sending down punishment—are all matters that accept the mediation of intermediaries like angels and other causes. This is in contrast to the purpose (الغرض) of creation and origination, for it is a matter exclusive to Allah, the Glorified, in which no one shares with Him.

His saying: "except that they may worship Me (إِلَّا لِيَعْبُدُونِ)" is an exception from a negation. There is no doubt in its apparent meaning that creation has a purpose (غرض), and that this purpose is worship (العبادة) — the act of the servants being worshippers of Allah, not His being worshipped. For He said: "that they may worship Me (ليعبدون)" and did not say: "so that I may worship" or "so that I may be worshipped by them."

Furthermore, a purpose (الغرض), whatever it may be, is something by which the possessor of the purpose attains perfection (يستكمل به) and through which his need is fulfilled. Yet Allah, the Glorified, has no deficiency in Him nor any need, such that He would seek perfection or the fulfillment of a need through it. From another perspective, an act that does not terminate in a purpose for its agent is a vain and foolish act. From this, it is concluded that He, the Glorified, has a purpose in His act which is His very Essence, not a purpose external to Him; and that His act has a purpose that returns to the act itself—which is a perfection for the act, not for its Agent. Thus, worship (العبادة) is a purpose for the creation of man and a perfection returning to him—both it and the effects that follow it, such as mercy, forgiveness, and so on. If worship had a purpose, such as the gnosis (المعرفة) attained through it and sincerity (الخلوص) for Allah, then that would be the ultimate purpose (الغرض الأقصى) and worship would be an intermediate purpose.

If you say: What you mentioned regarding interpreting the "Lām" (لـ) in "that they may worship Me (لِيَعْبُدُونِ)" as indicating purpose (الغرض) is contradicted by His saying, the Exalted: لا يَزالُونَ مُخْتَلِفِينَ إِلَّا مَنْ رَحِمَ رَبُّكَ وَلِذلِكَ خَلَقَهُمْ "...they will not cease to differ, except those on whom your Lord has mercy—and for that did He create them" (11:118-119), and His saying: وَلَقَدْ ذَرَأْنا لِجَهَنَّمَ كَثِيراً مِنَ الْجِنِّ وَالْإِنْسِ "Certainly We have wonnowed for hell many of the jinn and humans" (7:179). For the apparent meaning of the first verse is that the purpose of creation is difference (الاختلاف), and the apparent meaning of the second is that the purpose of creating many jinn and humans is entry into Hell. Therefore, there is no choice but to abandon interpreting the "Lām" as purpose and instead interpret it as the final outcome (الغاية).

I say: As for the first verse, the indication therein is toward mercy (الرحمة) rather than difference. As for the second verse, the "Lām" therein is for purpose, but it is a secondary purpose (غرض تبعي) and by secondary intent (بالقصد الثاني), not a primary purpose (غرض أصلي) and by primary intent (بالقصد الأول). Detailed discussion on the interpretation of these two verses has already preceded.

If you say: If the "Lām" in "that they may worship Me (لِيَعْبُدُونِ)" were for purpose, then worship would be the purpose of the Exalted intended from creation. It is impossible for His intended purpose to fail His will (إرادته). However, it is known and witnessed by sight that many of them do not worship Him, the Exalted. This is a strong proof that the "Lām" in the verse is not for purpose, or that it is for purpose but what is meant by worship is existential worship (العبادة التكوينية), as in His saying: وَإِنْ مِنْ شَيْءٍ إِلَّا يُسَبِّحُ بِحَمْدِهِ "...and there is not a thing but celebrates His praise" (17:44).

Or, that what is meant by creating them for worship is creating them in a manner suitable (على وجه صالح) for worshipping Allah, by making them possessors of choice, intellect, and capability. Treating potentiality and readiness (الصلاحية والاستعداد) as equivalent to actuality (الفعلية) is a common metaphor, as it is said: "Cattle were created for plowing," and "The house for dwelling."

I say: This objection is based on the "Lām" in "the jinn and the humans (الْجِنَّ وَالْإِنْسَ)" being for exhaustiveness (الاستغراق), such that the failure of the purpose in some individuals would contradict it and constitute a failure of the purpose. However, it is apparent that the "Lām" in both is for the genus (الجنس) rather than exhaustiveness. Thus, the existence of worship in the species in general constitutes the fulfillment of the purpose; its failure in some individuals does not harm it. Yes, if worship were removed from all individuals, that would be a nullification of the purpose. Allah, the Glorified, has a purpose in the species just as He has a purpose in the individual.

As for interpreting worship as existential worship (العبادة التكوينية), this is weakened by the fact that it is a state common to all creatures; there would be no reason to specify it to the jinn and humans. Furthermore, the context is one of rebuking the disbelievers for abandoning legislative worship (العبادة التشريعية) and threatening them for denying the Resurrection, the Reckoning, and the Requital—all of which pertain to legislative worship, not existential.

As for interpreting worship as suitability and readiness (الصلوح والاستعداد)—meaning that the purpose of creating the jinn and humans is that they be such that they are suitable for worship and ready for it, or for the attachment of legislative commands and prohibitions—this is weakened by the fact that it is clear that suitability and readiness are only sought for the sake of the actuality (الفعلية) to which they relate. If the purpose sought from their creation were that they be suitable for worship or for the attachment of legislative commands and prohibitions, then the purpose would have related first to the actuality of their worship, and then to the suitability and readiness by virtue of being a preliminary (المقدمية).

Thus, in interpreting worship as suitability and readiness, there is an admission that the purpose of creation, primarily and essentially (أولا وبالذات), is worship itself, and then suitability and readiness. Thus, the objection returns, if there were an objection at all.

The truth is that the "Lām" in "the jinn and the humans (الْجِنَّ وَالْإِنْسَ)" is for the genus, not exhaustiveness, and what is meant is worship itself, not suitability and readiness. Even if what were meant were suitability and readiness for worship, that would be a lower purpose sought for the sake of a higher purpose, which is worship. Just as worship itself—in the sense of the actions the servant performs with the limbs, such as standing, bowing, prostrating, and the like—is a purpose sought for the sake of another purpose, which is standing before the Lord of the worlds with the lowliness of servitude (ذلة العبودية) and the poverty of pure ownership (فقر المملوكية المحضة) over against absolute Might (العزة المطلقة) and pure Self-sufficiency (الغنى المحض). This is perhaps derived from His saying, the Exalted: قُلْ ما يَعْبَؤُا بِكُمْ رَبِّي لَوْ لا دُعاؤُكُمْ "Say, ‘My Lord would not care for you were it not for your prayer’" (25:77), where He substituted worship with prayer (دعاء).

The reality of worship (حقيقة العبادة) is the servant placing himself in the station of lowliness and servitude and directing his face toward the station of his Lord. This is what is meant by those who interpreted worship as gnosis (المعرفة)—meaning the gnosis attained through worship.

Thus, the reality of worship is the ultimate purpose (الغرض الأقصى) of creation, which is that the servant detaches himself from his own self and from everything else, and remembers his Lord.

This is what reflection yields regarding His saying, the Exalted: وَما خَلَقْتُ الْجِنَّ وَالْإِنْسَ إِلَّا لِيَعْبُدُونِ. Perhaps the mention of the jinn before the humans is due to their creation preceding that of humans; He, the Exalted, said: وَالْجَانَّ خَلَقْناهُ مِنْ قَبْلُ مِنْ نارِ السَّمُومِ "And the jinn We created before from a smokeless fire" (15:27).
